summary: I met Lonnie Pondly, a colored preacher who lived in a small two-room house down a white sandy lane. He was born in 1859 in Madison County, Georgia, and was once a slave. Lonnie had a good education for a negro of his time and shared vivid memories of his life under slavery, his masters, and the hardships faced during and after the Civil War. He also spoke about his experiences growing up, learning to read and write, and eventually becoming a teacher and preacher.

Madison County, Georgia, is where I was born and spent my early years as a slave. It was about six miles from Danielsville, and the plantations there were where my family and I lived and worked. This county was home to the Lester and Munn families, who owned the plantations where I was raised. It was a place marked by the struggles of slavery but also by the community and culture we built despite those hardships.

Danielsville, Georgia, was the nearby town about six miles from where I was born in Madison County. It was close to the plantations where my family lived, including those owned by the Lesters and Jimmie Munn. The Danielsville Road was a familiar place, especially since my father's master lived there. It was part of the landscape of my childhood and the life of the enslaved community around me.

Nancy Lester was my young mistress during my time as a slave. She was kind to us children, often paying us to help her with small tasks like bringing water or gathering bark. She cared for us deeply and never allowed anyone, not even our own parents, to whip us. Her kindness made our childhood happier despite the hardships of slavery.

John Lester was my master during slavery. He was a tough man who once told me I would never amount to anything when I refused to hoe cotton. Despite his sternness, he cared about the health and well-being of his slaves, wanting them to grow up strong and healthy. He was part of the Lester family who owned the plantation where I lived.

Jimmie Munn was my father's master, living on the Danielsville Road. My father had to get a pass from Mr. Jimmie to visit my mother since they were on different plantations. If he was caught without a pass, he faced severe punishment, including twenty-five lashes. Mr. Jimmie represented the strict control slave owners had over the lives of their slaves.

Sallie Lester was the daughter of my mistress and sometimes got mad at us children for trifling. She would try to whip us, but we would call for Old Mistress, who always stopped her. Miss Sallie’s anger was short-lived, and she never really harmed us. She was part of the Lester family that owned the plantation where I grew up.

Uncle Harry was a respected man in our community and was made a deacon by the preacher. He would pass the bread and wine during church services, which we attended together with both white and colored people sitting on different sides. Uncle Harry was clever; for example, he hid his watch under the woodpile to keep it from the Yankees. He was a symbol of strength and resilience among us.

The Yankees came through our area during the Civil War, and their presence was both frightening and disruptive. They took our horses, money, and valuables, often mistreating my mistress and others. I remember one Yankee cutting open my mistress’s dress to find gold and silver. They also tortured Mr. Franklin, my master's older brother, to find hidden money. Their arrival marked a harsh and violent period for us.

Mr. Franklin was my master's older brother who suffered terribly at the hands of the Yankees. They hung him by his toes and neck, trying to force him to reveal where he hid his money. Even when they threatened to bore into his head, he refused to tell until he begged them to stop and finally revealed the hiding place. He was a brave man who protected his family's valuables despite the torture.

Augusta, Georgia, was a distant place we would travel to with ox carts, taking about two weeks to make the round trip. We hauled cotton there, which was a slow and difficult journey. It was a significant destination for selling cotton and obtaining supplies like shoes and dresses. The trip to Augusta was part of the hard work and trade life on the plantation.

New Grove, Georgia, was where I lived after marrying Julia Johnson. It was the place where I settled down and dedicated my life to God. I worked hard in the fields there, picking cotton and managing my land and animals. New Grove marked a new chapter in my life after the hardships of slavery and the war.

Madison County, Georgia, was my birthplace and the site of my early life as a slave. It was home to the plantations of the Lester family and others who owned the land where my family lived. This county witnessed my childhood, the hardships of slavery, and the transition to freedom after the Civil War. It holds many memories of both pain and resilience.
